content:
# Exodus Chapter 16 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~42r–43v (PDF pp. 79–80); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 57–58 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_016_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_016_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Whole Community Grumbles (vv. 1–3)

**1** The whole Israelite community set out from Elim, and they came to the wilderness of Sin, which lies between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after they had left Egypt.

**2** There in the wilderness, the whole Israelite community complained bitterly against Moses and Aaron.

**3** The Israelites said to them, "If only YHWH had let us die in Egypt, where we sat by pots full of meat and ate bread until we were stuffed! Instead you have dragged us out into this wilderness to starve this whole assembly to death."

---

### YHWH Promises Bread from Heaven (vv. 4–8)

**4** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Look — I am going to rain down bread from heaven for you. The people are to go out each day and gather just enough for that day. In this way I will test them: will they follow my instructions, or not?"

**5** "On the sixth day, when they prepare what they have brought in, it will turn out to be twice as much as they gather on the other days."

**6** So Moses and Aaron said to all the Israelites, "By evening, you will know that it was YHWH who brought you out of Egypt. **7** And by morning, you will see the glory of YHWH — because he has heard your complaints against him. Who are we, that you should complain against us?"

**8** Then Moses said, "YHWH will give you meat to eat in the evening and all the bread you want in the morning, because YHWH has heard the complaints you are directing against him. Who are we? Your complaints are not really against us — they are against YHWH."

---

### The Glory of YHWH Appears (vv. 9–12)

**9** Moses said to Aaron, "Tell the whole Israelite community: 'Come into the presence of YHWH, for he has heard your complaints.'"

**10** While Aaron was speaking to the whole community, they turned and looked toward the wilderness — and there was the glory of YHWH, appearing in the cloud.

**11** YHWH spoke to Moses: **12** "I have heard the complaints of the Israelites. Tell them: 'At twilight you will eat meat, and in the morning you will be filled with bread — and you will know that I am YHWH your God.'"

---

### Quail and Manna — "What Is It?" (vv. 13–15)

**13** That evening, quail came and covered the camp. And in the morning, there was a layer of dew all around the camp. **14** When the dew evaporated, there on the surface of the wilderness lay something fine and flaky, as fine as frost on the ground.

**15** When the Israelites saw it, they said to each other, "*Man hu?*" — "What is it?" — because they did not know what it was. Moses said to them, "This is the bread that YHWH has given you to eat."

---

### Instructions for Gathering (vv. 16–21)

**16** "Here is what YHWH has commanded: Each person is to gather as much as they need — one omer per person — according to the number of people in your household."

**17** The Israelites did so. Some gathered more, some less. **18** But when they measured it out by the omer, the one who had gathered much did not have too much, and the one who had gathered little did not have too little. Each person ended up with exactly what they needed.

**19** Then Moses said to them, "No one is to keep any of it until morning."

**20** But some of them did not listen to Moses. They kept part of it until morning — and it bred worms and began to stink. Moses was angry with them.

**21** So each morning they gathered it, each person according to their need. And when the sun grew hot, it melted away.

---

### The Sabbath — A Holy Rest (vv. 22–30)

**22** On the sixth day, they gathered twice as much food — two omers per person. When all the leaders of the community came and reported this to Moses, **23** he said to them, "This is what YHWH has spoken: Tomorrow is a day of complete rest, a holy Sabbath dedicated to YHWH. Bake what you want to bake and boil what you want to boil today, and set aside whatever is left over — keep it until morning."

**24** So they set it aside until morning, as Moses had commanded — and it did not stink, and no worms appeared in it.

**25** Moses said, "Eat it today, for today is a Sabbath to YHWH. Today you will not find any on the ground. **26** Six days you are to gather it, but on the seventh day — the Sabbath — there will be none."

**27** Nevertheless, on the seventh day some of the people went out to gather — and they found nothing.

**28** YHWH said to Moses, "How long will you refuse to keep my commands and my instructions? **29** Understand this: YHWH has given you the Sabbath. That is why on the sixth day he gives you bread for two days. Everyone is to stay where they are — no one is to leave their place on the seventh day."

**30** So the people rested on the seventh day.

---

### The Manna Described and Preserved (vv. 31–36)

**31** The Israelites called the substance *man*. It was white, like coriander seed, and it tasted like wafers made with honey.

**32** Moses said, "This is what YHWH has commanded: 'Keep one full omer of it for your descendants, so that they can see the bread I fed you in the wilderness when I brought you out of Egypt.'"

**33** Moses said to Aaron, "Take a jar and put one full omer of manna in it, and place it before YHWH, to be kept for your descendants."

**34** As YHWH had commanded Moses, Aaron placed it before the Testimony, to be preserved.

**35** The Israelites ate the manna for forty years, until they came to a land where they could settle — they ate the manna until they reached the border of the land of Canaan.

**36** (An omer is one-tenth of an ephah.)

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — THE WILDERNESS OF SIN (Heb. *vayyis'u me'Elim vayyavo'u kol-adat benei-Yisra'el el-midbar-Sin asher bein-Elim uvein Sinai bachamishah asar yom lachodesh hasheni letse'tam me'erets Mitsrayim*, וַיִּסְעוּ֙ מֵֽאֵילִ֔ם וַיָּבֹ֜אוּ כָּל־עֲדַ֤ת בְּנֵֽי־יִשְׂרָאֵל֙ אֶל־מִדְבַּר־סִ֔ין אֲשֶׁ֥ר בֵּין־אֵילִ֖ם וּבֵ֣ין סִינָ֑י בַּחֲמִשָּׁ֨ה עָשָׂ֥ר יוֹם֙ לַחֹ֣דֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִ֔י לְצֵאתָ֖ם מֵאֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָֽיִם):** The name *midbar-Sin* (מדבר סין) is unrelated to the English word "sin"; it is a geographical name, probably connected to the Semitic moon-god Sin (Akkadian *Suen/Sin*), whose worship was widespread in the desert regions between Egypt and Canaan. The wilderness of Sin should not be confused with the wilderness of Sinai (סיני), though the names share a consonantal root and the location is described as "between Elim and Sinai" — placing this event geographically and narratively on the road to Sinai. The precise date — the fifteenth day of the second month — places the event exactly one month after the exodus (which occurred on the fifteenth of the first month, 12:6, 31). One month of freedom, and the people are ready to return to slavery for the sake of a full stomach.

The phrase *kol-adat benei-Yisra'el* (כל עדת בני ישראל, "the whole congregation of the children of Israel") uses *edah* (עדה, "congregation, assembly, community"), a term that emphasizes the people's collective identity as a gathered body. The complaint is not from a faction but from the entire community — the grumbling is unanimous.

**vv.2-3 — "WOULD THAT WE HAD DIED IN EGYPT" — THE NOSTALGIA FOR SLAVERY (Heb. *vayyillonu kol-adat benei-Yisra'el al-Mosheh ve'al-Aharon bammidbar; vayyomru aleihem benei Yisra'el mi-yitten mutenu veyad-YHWH be'erets Mitsrayim beshivtenu al-sir habasar be'okhlenu lechem lasova ki-hotse'tem otanu el-hammidbar hazzeh lehamit et-kol-haqqahal hazzeh bara'av*, וַיִּלּ֜וֹנוּ כָּל־עֲדַ֧ת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֛ל עַל־מֹשֶׁ֥ה וְעַֽל־אַהֲרֹ֖ן בַּמִּדְבָּ֑ר וַיֹּאמְר֨וּ אֲלֵהֶ֜ם בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל מִֽי־יִתֵּ֨ן מוּתֵ֤נוּ בְיַד־יְהוָה֙ בְּאֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרַ֔יִם בְּשִׁבְתֵּ֨נוּ֙ עַל־סִ֣יר הַבָּשָׂ֔ר בְּאָכְלֵ֥נוּ לֶ֖חֶם לָשֹׂ֑בַע כִּֽי־הוֹצֵאתֶ֤ם אֹתָ֨נוּ֙ אֶל־הַמִּדְבָּ֣ר הַזֶּ֔ה לְהָמִ֛ית אֶת־כָּל־הַקָּהָ֥ל הַזֶּ֖ה בָּרָעָֽב):** The verb *vayyillonu* (וילנו, from *lun*, לון, "to murmur, to grumble, to lodge a complaint") appears again — the same verb from 15:24 at Marah — but now escalated. At Marah, they complained about water; here they wish they were dead. The construction *mi-yitten mutenu* (מי יתן מותנו, literally "who will give our dying?" = "would that we had died!") is an optative idiom — a wish for death. This is one of the most psychologically devastating utterances in the Hebrew Bible: people who were enslaved, beaten, and had their children murdered are now saying they wish they had died in Egypt, *by the hand of YHWH* (*beyad-YHWH*, ביד יהוה), sitting beside the *sir habasar* (סיר הבשר, "the pot of meat") and eating bread *lasova'* (לשבע, "to satiety, to fullness").

The irony is multilayered. First, there were no "pots of meat" for Hebrew slaves in Egypt — the memory is fabricated, a golden-age fantasy of a bondage that was actually brutal. Second, the word *lechem* (לחם, "bread") that they claim to have eaten to satiety in Egypt is precisely the word God will use in v.4 — "I will rain *lechem* from heaven." Third, they accuse Moses and Aaron of bringing them out *lehamit* (להמית, "to kill") the whole *qahal* (קהל, "assembly") with *ra'av* (רעב, "hunger, famine"). The charge is murder by starvation. The people reinterpret their liberation as a death sentence.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders *mi-yitten mutenu* as *ophelos apethanomen* (ὄφελος ἀπεθάνομεν, "would that we had died"), capturing the optative sense. The phrase *sir habasar* becomes *kreōn* (κρεῶν, "of meats") in the LXX tradition — the fleshpots become simply "meats."

**v.4 — "I WILL RAIN DOWN BREAD FROM HEAVEN" — *LECHEM MIN HASHAMAYIM* (Heb. *vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh hineni mamtir lakhem lechem min-hashamayim veyatsa ha'am velaqtu devar-yom beyomo lema'an anassenu hayelekh betorati im-lo*, וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יְהוָה֙ אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֔ה הִנְנִ֨י מַמְטִ֥יר לָכֶ֛ם לֶ֖חֶם מִן־הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם וְיָצָ֣א הָעָ֗ם וְלָֽקְט֛וּ דְּבַר־י֥וֹם בְּיוֹמ֖וֹ לְמַ֣עַן אֲנַסֶּ֔נּוּ הֲיֵלֵ֥ךְ בְּתוֹרָתִ֖י אִם־לֹֽא):** This verse is one of the most theologically loaded in the Torah. YHWH responds to the complaint not with rebuke but with provision. The verb *mamtir* (ממטיר, hiphil participle of *matar*, מטר, "to rain") — "I am about to cause rain" — casts the bread as precipitation, as weather, as a natural-supernatural event that comes from *hashamayim* (השמים, "the heavens/sky"). The phrase *lechem min-hashamayim* (לחם מן השמים, "bread from heaven") will echo across the entire biblical tradition: Nehemiah 9:15, Psalm 78:24, Psalm 105:40, and eventually John 6:31-35, where Jesus identifies himself as "the true bread from heaven." The concept is stunning: heaven's floor is earth's ceiling, and bread falls from the place where God dwells.

The phrase *devar-yom beyomo* (דבר יום ביומו, "the matter of a day in its day" = "the daily portion for each day") establishes the gathering principle: just enough for today, no more. This is not merely a logistical rule; it is a spiritual discipline. The people must trust God for tomorrow's bread tomorrow. Hoarding is forbidden — not because there is no surplus, but because the surplus would replace trust with security. The manna economy inverts Egypt's economy: in Egypt, stockpiling was power (cf. Pharaoh's granaries in Genesis 41); in the wilderness, stockpiling is faithlessness.

The clause *lema'an anassenu* (למען אנסנו, "in order that I may test them") reveals God's deeper purpose: the manna is a test (*nasah*, נסה, "to test, to prove, to try"). The question — *hayelekh betorati im-lo* (הילך בתורתי אם לא, "will they walk in my Torah or not?") — uses the word *Torah* (תורה, "instruction, law, teaching") before Sinai. Torah precedes Sinai. The instruction to gather daily is already *Torah* — divine instruction that requires obedient trust. The manna is a Torah-before-the-Torah.

**v.7 — THE GLORY OF YHWH — *KEVOD YHWH* (Heb. *uvoqer ure'item et-kevod YHWH beshom'o et-telunoteikhem al-YHWH venachnu mah ki tallinu alenu*, וּבֹ֗קֶר וּרְאִיתֶם֙ אֶת־כְּב֣וֹד יְהוָ֔ה בְּשָׁמְע֥וֹ אֶת־תְּלֻנֹּתֵיכֶ֖ם עַל־יְהוָ֑ה וְנַ֣חְנוּ מָ֔ה כִּ֥י תַלִּ֖ינוּ עָלֵֽינוּ):** The phrase *kevod YHWH* (כבוד יהוה, "the glory of YHWH") is one of the most important theological concepts in the Hebrew Bible. The word *kavod* (כבוד, from *kaved*, כבד, "to be heavy, weighty") means fundamentally "weightiness" — the tangible, impressive, overwhelming presence of YHWH made visible. When *kevod YHWH* appears, it is not a metaphor; it is a theophany — a visible manifestation of divine presence, typically associated with fire, light, and cloud (cf. 24:16-17; 40:34-35; Lev. 9:23; Num. 14:10; 1 Kings 8:11; Ezek. 1:28). The glory is what happens when heaven's weight presses into earth's space.

The question *venachnu mah* (ונחנו מה, "and what are we?") — repeated from v.7 to v.8 — is Moses' self-effacement. It echoes Moses' protest at the bush (*mi anokhi*, "who am I?" 3:11) and anticipates the great question of Psalm 8:4 (*mah-enosh*, "what is a human?"). Moses insists that the real target of Israel's complaint is not him but YHWH. This is theologically precise: to complain about the wilderness conditions is to complain about the God who led them there.

The LXX renders *kevod YHWH* as *doxan kyriou* (δόξαν κυρίου, "the glory of the Lord"), using *doxa* — the word that in secular Greek meant "opinion" or "reputation" but which the LXX transformed into the standard translation of *kavod*, thereby redefining it as visible, radiant divine majesty. This LXX usage of *doxa* became foundational for the New Testament's glory theology.

**v.10 — THE GLORY IN THE CLOUD (Heb. *vayehi kedabber Aharon el-kol-adat benei-Yisra'el vayyifnu el-hammidbar vehinneh kevod YHWH nir'ah be'anan*, וַיְהִ֗י כְּדַבֵּ֤ר אַהֲרֹן֙ אֶל־כָּל־עֲדַ֣ת בְּנֵֽי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל וַיִּפְנ֖וּ אֶל־הַמִּדְבָּ֑ר וְהִנֵּה֙ כְּב֣וֹד יְהוָ֔ה נִרְאָ֖ה בֶּעָנָֽן):** The narrative particle *vehinneh* (והנה, "and look! / and behold!") signals sudden, dramatic appearance — the reader is meant to see what the Israelites saw. The glory *nir'ah* (נראה, from *ra'ah*, ראה, "to see, to appear") — passive, "was seen, appeared" — manifests *be'anan* (בענן, "in the cloud"). The cloud (*anan*, ענן) has been guiding Israel since 13:21-22, but now the cloud is not merely a guide; it is a container for the *kavod*. The cloud mediates between the divine glory that would kill and the human eyes that cannot bear it. The cloud both reveals and conceals — you can see that God is present without seeing God directly. This paradox will intensify at Sinai (19:9, 16; 24:15-18) and become the defining characteristic of tabernacle theology (40:34-38).

**v.13 — QUAIL AND DEW (Heb. *vayehi va'erev vatta'al hasselav vatekhas et-hammachaneh uvaboqer hayetah shikhvat hattal saviv lammachaneh*, וַיְהִ֣י בָעֶ֔רֶב וַתַּ֣עַל הַשְּׂלָ֔ו וַתְּכַ֖ס אֶת־הַֽמַּחֲנֶ֑ה וּבַבֹּ֗קֶר הָֽיְתָה֙ שִׁכְבַ֣ת הַטַּ֔ל סָבִ֖יב לַֽמַּחֲנֶֽה):** The *selav* (שלו, "quail") is a migratory bird (Coturnix coturnix) that crosses the Sinai Peninsula in large flocks during spring and autumn migration. Exhausted from their flight across the Mediterranean, the birds often land in such numbers and in such a weakened state that they can be caught by hand. The text says the quail *vatta'al* (ותעל, from *alah*, עלה, "to go up, to come up") and *vatekhas* (ותכס, from *kasah*, כסה, "to cover") the camp — they ascended and blanketed the entire encampment. This is the evening provision, corresponding to God's promise of "meat at twilight" (v.12). The morning provision is introduced through the *shikhvat hattal* (שכבת הטל, "layer/lying of dew"), a delicate image — the manna arrives nestled in dew, as if heaven wraps its bread in moisture.

**vv.14-15 — *MAN HU* — "WHAT IS IT?" — THE NAMING OF MANNA (Heb. *vatta'al shikhvat hattal vehinneh al-penei hammidbar daq mechuspas daq kakkephor al-ha'arets; vayyir'u venei-Yisra'el vayyomru ish el-achiv man hu ki lo yade'u mah-hu vayyomer Mosheh aleihem hu hallecehm asher natan YHWH lakhem le'okhlah*, וַתַּ֖עַל שִׁכְבַ֣ת הַטָּ֑ל וְהִנֵּ֞ה עַל־פְּנֵ֤י הַמִּדְבָּר֙ דַּ֣ק מְחֻסְפָּ֔ס דַּ֥ק כַּכְּפֹ֖ר עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ וַיִּרְא֣וּ בְנֵֽי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל וַיֹּ֨אמְר֜וּ אִ֤ישׁ אֶל־אָחִיו֙ מָ֣ן ה֔וּא כִּ֛י לֹ֥א יָדְע֖וּ מַה־ה֑וּא וַיֹּ֤אמֶר מֹשֶׁה֙ אֲלֵהֶ֔ם ה֣וּא הַלֶּ֔חֶם אֲשֶׁ֨ר נָתַ֧ן יְהוָ֛ה לָכֶ֖ם לְאָכְלָֽה):** The description of the manna is precise and puzzling. It is *daq* (דק, "thin, fine, small") — repeated twice for emphasis — and *mechuspas* (מחספס), a word that appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible (*hapax legomenon*). Its meaning is uncertain; traditional translations include "flaky," "scale-like," "granular," or "crusty." The Aramaic Targumim interpret it as "exposed, uncovered." The simile *kakkephor* (ככפר, "like frost/hoarfrost") compares it to the thin layer of ice crystals that forms on cold surfaces — something white, delicate, and crystalline.

The exclamation *man hu* (מן הוא, "what is it?") is the folk etymology that gives manna its name. The word *man* (מן) is most likely an Aramaic or archaic Hebrew interrogative meaning "what?" — cognate with standard Hebrew *mah* (מה, "what?"). The people's question — *ish el-achiv* (איש אל אחיו, "each person to his brother/neighbor") — captures the collective bewilderment. They have never seen anything like this. It defies their categories. The substance has no name because it has no precedent. Moses' answer is theological rather than botanical: *hu hallecehm asher natan YHWH lakhem* (הוא הלחם אשר נתן יהוה לכם, "it is the bread that YHWH has given you"). He does not explain what it is; he explains where it comes from. Identity is defined by origin: this is heaven's bread.

The LXX renders *man hu* as *ti estin touto* (τί ἐστιν τοῦτο, "what is this?"), a straightforward translation of the question. But the Hebrew preserves the foreignness of the word *man* — a word from outside standard Hebrew, mirroring the substance's otherworldliness.

Various naturalistic identifications have been proposed: the secretion of the tamarisk insect (*Trabutina mannipara*), which produces sweet, white, flaky droplets on tamarisk branches in the Sinai; a lichen (*Lecanora esculenta*) blown by winds; or honeydew from plant lice. None of these fully matches the biblical description (especially the quantities, the daily regularity, the Sabbath exception, and the forty-year duration), suggesting that whatever natural phenomenon may underlie the tradition, the text presents the manna as a unique divine provision that exceeds natural explanation.

**v.16 — THE OMER — DAILY SUFFICIENCY (Heb. *zeh haddavar asher tsivvah YHWH liqtu mimmennu ish lefi okhlo omer lagulgolot mispar nafshoteikhem ish la'asher be'oholo tiqqachu*, זֶ֤ה הַדָּבָר֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר צִוָּ֣ה יְהוָ֔ה לִקְט֣וּ מִמֶּ֔נּוּ אִ֖ישׁ לְפִ֣י אָכְל֑וֹ עֹ֣מֶר לַגֻּלְגֹּ֗לֶת מִסְפַּר֙ נַפְשֹׁ֣תֵיכֶ֔ם אִ֛ישׁ לַאֲשֶׁ֥ר בְּאָהֳל֖וֹ תִּקָּֽחוּ):** The *omer* (עמר) is a unit of dry measure, defined in v.36 as one-tenth of an *ephah* (אֵיפָה), approximately 2.3 liters or about 2 quarts — enough grain or bread substance for one person's daily food. The phrase *lagulgolot* (לגלגלת, "per head," literally "per skull," from *gulgolet*, גלגלת, "skull") is a census term — the same word used in Numbers 1:2 for counting the population "by skulls." Each skull gets one omer. The phrase *lefi okhlo* (לפי אכלו, "according to one's eating/mouth") — *lefi* (לפי, "according to the mouth of") — means "in proportion to what each person can eat." The manna economy is radically egalitarian: no one gets more than they need, no one gets less. The rich cannot hoard; the poor do not starve.

**v.18 — THE MIRACLE OF SUFFICIENCY (Heb. *vayyamoddu va'omer velo he'edif hammarbeh vehamma'it lo hechsir ish lefi-okhlo laqatu*, וַיָּמֹ֣דּוּ בָעֹ֔מֶר וְלֹ֤א הֶעְדִּיף֙ הַמַּרְבֶּ֔ה וְהַמַּמְעִ֖יט לֹ֣א הֶחְסִ֑יר אִ֥ישׁ לְפִֽי־אָכְל֖וֹ לָקָֽטוּ):** This verse describes the manna's most mysterious quality: regardless of how much each person gathered, when they measured it, everyone had exactly one omer. The one who gathered much (*hammarbeh*, המרבה) had no surplus (*lo he'edif*, לא העדיף, "did not have excess," from *adaf*, עדף, "to be left over"); the one who gathered little (*hamma'it*, הממעיט, from *ma'at*, מעט, "to be few") had no deficiency (*lo hechsir*, לא החסיר, "did not lack," from *chaser*, חסר, "to lack, to decrease"). This is not merely an economic arrangement; it is a daily miracle that abolishes inequality. Paul quotes this verse in 2 Corinthians 8:15 as a model for economic sharing among Christian communities. The manna subverts every acquisitive instinct: you cannot accumulate divine provision. You can only receive today's portion today.

**vv.19-20 — THE FAILURE TO TRUST (Heb. *vayyomer Mosheh aleihem ish al-yoter mimmennu ad-boqer; velo-sham'u el-Mosheh vayyotiru anashim mimmennu ad-boqer vayyarum tola'im vayyiv'ash vayyiqtsof aleihem Mosheh*, וַיֹּ֥אמֶר מֹשֶׁ֖ה אֲלֵהֶ֑ם אִ֕ישׁ אַל־יוֹתֵ֥ר מִמֶּ֖נּוּ עַד־בֹּֽקֶר וְלֹא־שָׁמְע֣וּ אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֗ה וַיּוֹתִ֨רוּ אֲנָשִׁ֤ים מִמֶּ֨נּוּ֙ עַד־בֹּ֔קֶר וַיָּ֥רֻם תּוֹלָעִ֖ים וַיִּבְאַ֑שׁ וַיִּקְצֹ֥ף עֲלֵהֶ֖ם מֹשֶֽׁה):** The verb *vayyotiru* (ויותרו, from *yatar*, יתר, "to leave over, to have remaining") describes the sin: some people kept manna overnight. The consequence is immediate and visceral: *vayyarum tola'im* (וירם תולעים, "it bred worms," from *rum*, רום, "to rise up" — the worms "arose" in the bread) and *vayyiv'ash* (ויבאש, from *ba'ash*, באש, "to stink, to become foul"). The rot is not natural decay but divine enforcement: hoarded manna becomes revolting. The message is unmistakable: bread from heaven cannot be stockpiled. Each morning is a fresh act of trust. Those who try to secure tomorrow's provision today find that their security rots in their hands.

Moses' response — *vayyiqtsof* (ויקצף, from *qatsaf*, קצף, "to be angry, to be furious") — is rare. Moses is not merely annoyed; he is angry. The disobedience is not a logistical error; it is a failure of faith.

**vv.22-23 — THE FIRST SABBATH LEGISLATION (Heb. *vayehi bayyom hashishshi laqtu lechem mishneh shenei ha'omer la'echad vayyavo'u kol-nesi'ei ha'edah vayyaggidu leMosheh; vayyomer aleihem hu asher dibber YHWH shabbaton shabbat-qodesh laYHWH machar et asher-tofu efu ve'et asher-tevashlu bashshelu ve'et kol-ha'odef hannichu lakhem lemishmeret ad-habboqer*, וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁ֗י לָֽקְט֥וּ לֶ֨חֶם֙ מִשְׁנֶ֔ה שְׁנֵ֥י הָעֹ֖מֶר לָאֶחָ֑ד וַיָּבֹ֨אוּ֙ כָּל־נְשִׂיאֵ֣י הָֽעֵדָ֔ה וַיַּגִּ֖ידוּ לְמֹשֶֽׁה וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֲלֵהֶ֗ם ה֚וּא אֲשֶׁ֣ר דִּבֶּ֣ר יְהוָ֔ה שַׁבָּת֧וֹן שַׁבַּת־קֹ֛דֶשׁ לַיהוָ֖ה מָחָ֑ר אֵ֣ת אֲשֶׁר־תֹּאפ֞וּ אֵפ֗וּ וְאֵ֤ת אֲשֶׁר־תְּבַשְּׁלוּ֙ בַּשֵּׁ֔לוּ וְאֵ֨ת כָּל־הָעֹדֵ֜ף הַנִּ֥יחוּ לָכֶ֛ם לְמִשְׁמֶ֖רֶת עַד־הַבֹּֽקֶר):** This is the first appearance of the Sabbath in legislative form in the Hebrew Bible. The word *shabbaton* (שבתון, "a sabbath-rest, a complete rest") is an intensified form of *shabbat* (שבת), and the doubled phrase *shabbaton shabbat-qodesh* (שבתון שבת קדש, "a rest-of-resting, a holy sabbath") piles emphasis upon emphasis: this is not merely a day off but a holy cessation. The root *shavat* (שבת, "to cease, to stop, to rest") is the same root used of God in Genesis 2:2-3, tying the wilderness Sabbath to the creation Sabbath. By resting on the seventh day, Israel imitates the Creator.

The critical innovation: on the sixth day, the manna doubles (*lechem mishneh*, לחם משנה, "double bread") and — unlike every other night — the overnight portion does not rot (v.24). The manna itself observes the Sabbath. The natural order bends to the sacred calendar. God teaches Israel the Sabbath rhythm not through commandment alone but through the behavior of bread: six days it rots if kept; on the sixth day it keeps. The food itself becomes Torah.

The *nesi'ei ha'edah* (נשיאי העדה, "the leaders/chieftains of the congregation") come to Moses with the anomaly — they have found double portions and do not know what to make of it. Moses' response reveals that God has already spoken (*hu asher dibber YHWH*, הוא אשר דבר יהוה, "this is what YHWH has spoken"). The double portion is not an accident; it is the design.

**vv.28-29 — "HOW LONG WILL YOU REFUSE?" (Heb. *vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh ad-anah me'antem lishmor mitsvotai vetorotai; re'u ki-YHWH natan lakhem hashabbat al-ken hu noten lakhem bayyom hashishshi lechem yomayim shevu ish tachtav al-yetse ish mimmeqomo bayyom hashevi'i*, וַיֹּ֥אמֶר יְהוָ֖ה אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֑ה עַד־אָ֗נָה מֵֽאַנְתֶּ֛ם לִשְׁמֹ֥ר מִצְוֺתַ֖י וְתוֹרֹתָֽי רְא֕וּ כִּֽי־יְהוָ֖ה נָתַ֣ן לָכֶ֑ם הַשַּׁבָּ֗ת עַל־כֵּ֨ן ה֣וּא נֹתֵ֤ן לָכֶם֙ בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁ֔י לֶ֖חֶם יוֹמָ֑יִם שְׁב֣וּ ׀ אִ֣ישׁ תַּחְתָּ֗יו אַל־יֵ֥צֵא אִ֛ישׁ מִמְּקֹמ֖וֹ בַּיּ֥וֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִֽי):** YHWH's question — *ad-anah me'antem* (עד אנה מאנתם, "how long do you refuse?") — is striking because it is addressed to Moses (*el-Mosheh*) but uses the plural "you" (*me'antem*), indicating that the question is directed through Moses to the people. The verb *me'antem* (מאנתם, from *ma'en*, מאן, "to refuse, to be unwilling") implies willful resistance, not ignorance. The plural *torotai* (תורתי, "my instructions/teachings") — note the plural *torot* rather than the singular *Torah* — suggests multiple specific instructions, not yet the singular body of Sinai law.

The command *shevu ish tachtav* (שבו איש תחתיו, "let each person stay in their place") — from *yashav* (ישב, "to sit, to dwell, to remain") — establishes the Sabbath as not merely cessation of work but physical staying-put. The Sabbath restricts movement: *al-yetse ish mimmeqomo* (אל יצא איש ממקמו, "let no one go out from their place"). This will later become the basis for the rabbinic concept of *techum shabbat* (תחום שבת, "Sabbath boundary") — the limit of how far one may walk on the Sabbath (typically 2,000 cubits from one's dwelling). The Sabbath is given (*natan*, נתן, "to give") as a gift — *YHWH natan lakhem hashabbat* (יהוה נתן לכם השבת, "YHWH has given you the Sabbath"). It is not a burden imposed but a gift bestowed.

**v.31 — THE DESCRIPTION OF MANNA (Heb. *vayyiqre'u veit-Yisra'el et-shemo man vehu kezera gad lavan veta'amo ketsappichit bidvash*, וַיִּקְרְא֧וּ בֵית־יִשְׂרָאֵ֛ל אֶת־שְׁמ֖וֹ מָ֑ן וְה֗וּא כְּזֶ֤רַע גַּד֙ לָבָ֔ן וְטַעְמ֖וֹ כְּצַפִּיחִ֥ת בִּדְבָֽשׁ):** The Israelites formally name the substance *man* (מן) — the bewildered question becomes the proper name. It is compared to *zera gad* (זרע גד, "coriander seed") — *gad* (גד) being the coriander plant (*Coriandrum sativum*), whose small, round, pale seeds are a common spice throughout the ancient Near East. Numbers 11:7 will add that it looked like *bdolach* (בדלח, "bdellium"), a resinous gum. The color is *lavan* (לבן, "white") — heaven's bread is white, pure, unblemished. The taste is *ketsappichit bidvash* (כצפיחת בדבש, "like a wafer with/in honey"). The word *tsappichit* (צפיחת) appears only here and in the parallel description — probably a thin, flat cake or wafer. The honey (*devash*, דבש) could be bee honey or date syrup (*dibs*), both called *devash* in Hebrew. The description is sensory and precise: small, white, round, sweet — bread that tastes like dessert.

**vv.33-34 — MANNA BEFORE THE TESTIMONY (Heb. *vayyomer Mosheh el-Aharon qach tsintseneth achat veten-shammah melo-ha'omer man vehannach oto lifnei YHWH lemishmeret ledoroteikhem; ka'asher tsivvah YHWH el-Mosheh vayyannnichehu Aharon lifnei ha'edut lemishmaret*, וַיֹּ֨אמֶר מֹשֶׁ֜ה אֶֽל־אַהֲרֹ֗ן קַ֚ח צִנְצֶ֣נֶת אַחַ֔ת וְתֶן־שָׁ֥מָּה מְלֹֽא־הָעֹ֖מֶר מָ֑ן וְהַנַּ֤ח אֹתוֹ֙ לִפְנֵ֣י יְהוָ֔ה לְמִשְׁמֶ֖רֶת לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶֽם כַּאֲשֶׁ֛ר צִוָּ֥ה יְהוָ֖ה אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֑ה וַיַּנִּיחֵ֧הוּ אַהֲרֹ֛ן לִפְנֵ֥י הָעֵדֻ֖ת לְמִשְׁמָֽרֶת):** The word *tsintseneth* (צנצנת, "jar, container") appears only here in the Hebrew Bible — another *hapax legomenon*. The LXX renders it *stamnon chrysoun* (στάμνον χρυσοῦν, "a golden jar"), adding the detail "golden" that is absent from the Hebrew. This LXX addition is reflected in Hebrews 9:4, which describes "the golden jar holding the manna" as one of the items inside the ark of the covenant.

The jar is to be placed *lifnei YHWH* (לפני יהוה, "before YHWH") — that is, in the sacred space where YHWH's presence dwells. Verse 34 specifies *lifnei ha'edut* (לפני העדת, "before the Testimony"), referring to the *edut* (עדות, "testimony, witness") — the tablets of the covenant law that will be placed in the ark (25:16, 21). This is a proleptic reference: the ark has not yet been built, the tablets have not yet been given, but the text narrates from the perspective of completed sacred architecture. The manna-jar will eventually rest beside the tablets inside (or before) the ark — physical evidence that God fed Israel in the wilderness, a memorial of divine provision kept in the holiest place on earth.

The command *lemishmeret ledoroteikhem* (למשמרת לדרתיכם, "as a keeping/guard for your generations") uses *mishmeret* (משמרת, from *shamar*, שמר, "to keep, to guard, to watch over") — the manna is to be "guarded" for future generations as testimony. The bread that could not be kept overnight is now kept forever. What rotted when hoarded for selfish security endures when preserved for sacred witness.

**v.35 — FORTY YEARS OF MANNA (Heb. *uvenei Yisra'el akhlu et-hamman arba'im shanah ad-bo'am el-erets noshevet et-hamman akhlu ad-bo'am el-qetseh erets Kena'an*, וּבְנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל אָכְל֤וּ אֶת־הַמָּן֙ אַרְבָּעִ֣ים שָׁנָ֔ה עַד־בֹּאָ֖ם אֶל־אֶ֣רֶץ נוֹשָׁ֑בֶת אֶת־הַמָּן֙ אָֽכְל֔וּ עַד־בֹּאָ֖ם אֶל־קְצֵ֥ה אֶ֥רֶץ כְּנָֽעַן):** This verse telescopes the entire wilderness period into a single statement. The number *arba'im shanah* (ארבעים שנה, "forty years") is the Bible's characteristic number for a complete period of testing or transition (cf. the flood's forty days, Moses' forty days on Sinai, Israel's forty years, Elijah's forty-day journey, Jesus' forty days in the wilderness). The manna continues until Israel reaches *erets noshevet* (ארץ נושבת, "an inhabited/settled land") — a land where the ground itself produces food, making heaven's bread unnecessary. Joshua 5:12 records the exact moment the manna ceased: the day after Passover in the promised land, when they ate the produce of Canaan. The transition from manna to agriculture is the transition from miraculous provision to covenantal responsibility — from bread that falls from heaven to bread that must be worked from the earth.

**v.36 — THE OMER GLOSS (Heb. *veha'omer asirit ha'efah hu*, וְהָעֹ֕מֶר עֲשִׂרִ֥ית הָאֵיפָ֖ה הֽוּא):** This parenthetical note explains the *omer* for readers who no longer use the measurement in daily life — evidence that the text was edited for an audience chronologically distant from the wilderness period. The *ephah* (אֵיפָה) is approximately 22 liters (roughly 5.8 gallons), making one omer about 2.2 liters. This kind of explanatory gloss is common in biblical narrative (cf. Gen. 23:2, "Hebron, that is, Kiriath-arba"; Ruth 4:7, explaining the sandal custom) and signals the editorial hand that shaped the final text.

---

**The theology of Exodus 16 — bread, trust, and the rhythm of grace:**

This chapter is one of the most theologically dense narratives in the Torah. It introduces themes that reverberate across the entire biblical tradition: the nature of divine provision, the relationship between obedience and trust, the institution of the Sabbath, and the meaning of "enough."

**1. The manna as anti-Egypt.** Egypt was the land of abundance — granaries, fleshpots, bread to satiety. But Egypt's abundance was built on slavery. The manna inverts Egypt's economy at every point: instead of slave labor producing surplus for Pharaoh, divine gift provides sufficiency for everyone. Instead of stockpiling (Joseph's granary system, Genesis 41), the people must gather only what they need today. Instead of hierarchical distribution where Pharaoh controls the food supply, the manna falls equally for all. The wilderness economy is an economic revolution: God replaces Pharaoh as the provider, and the provision is structured to prevent the re-emergence of Egypt's exploitative patterns.

**2. The test of daily trust.** The manna's most radical feature is its refusal to be stored. Bread that rots overnight forces daily dependence on the Giver. This is not scarcity — God gives abundantly — but it is a specific kind of abundance that cannot be converted into security. The manna trains Israel out of the hoarding instinct that Egypt cultivated and into a rhythm of daily trust. The prayer Jesus teaches — "give us this day our daily bread" (Matt. 6:11) — is a direct echo of the manna economy. The Greek word *epiousios* (ἐπιούσιος, usually translated "daily") is itself a *hapax legomenon* in Greek literature, as mysterious as the manna it references.

**3. The Sabbath embedded in creation.** Exodus 16 presents the Sabbath not as a Sinai commandment (that will come in chapter 20) but as a pattern built into the structure of provision itself. The manna doubles on the sixth day and does not rot overnight — the Sabbath is inscribed in the behavior of bread before it is inscribed on tablets of stone. This suggests that the Sabbath is not an arbitrary rule but a rhythm inherent in the created order. God does not merely command rest; God provides for rest by restructuring the food supply to accommodate it. The Sabbath is a gift (*natan*, v.29) before it is a command.

**4. *Kevod YHWH* — glory in the wilderness.** The appearance of the glory of YHWH in the cloud (v.10) in response to the people's complaint is theologically startling. The people grumble, and God responds not with punishment but with presence. The *kavod* appears to announce provision, not judgment. This establishes a pattern that will define the tabernacle theology: YHWH's glory dwells among a complaining, faithless people — not because they deserve it, but because YHWH chooses to be present. The glory is not earned; it is given, like the manna itself.

**5. The jar before the Testimony.** The command to preserve an omer of manna before the ark (vv.33-34) transforms perishable food into permanent testimony. The substance that rotted when hoarded for private security endures forever when placed before God as public witness. The jar of manna joins the tablets of the law and (later) Aaron's budding rod (Num. 17:10) as the three objects kept in or before the ark — three witnesses to God's provision, God's instruction, and God's choice of leadership. The manna-jar says to every future generation: there was a time when God fed his people with bread from heaven, one day at a time, for forty years, and it was enough.

— *PNEUMA*
